b' Harris Money Troubles, \xe2\x80\x9926 Fantasies & Polling P.T.S.D.
Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. Tonight, I\xe2\x80\x99ve got some fresh reporting on the remarkable debt the Harris campaign racked up in its final, frantic weeks, and how they plan to pay it down. Plus, dispatches from Abby Livingston on the midterm hype already building around Abigail Spanberger (the Dems\xe2\x80\x99 Glenn Youngkin?) and Mike Lawler (the next Lee Zeldin?) and a post-Selzer post-mortem from John Heilemann. \xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c

Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. I\xe2\x80\x99m Tara Palmeri .

If the election result is still sinking in, you\xe2\x80\x99re not alone. I can report that there\xe2\x80\x99s been a bit of a delayed reaction for both the candidates and their teams, as well. Democrats are busy shivving each other inside the tent, playing the cable news blame game, or quietly drowning their sorrows far from Le Dip. Even Trump \xe2\x80\x99s team, giddy as they may be, can hardly believe they swept the swing states, too. They outperformed their own expectations, internal models, and polling.

Now comes the name game: Will Ric Grenell get State, or will Sen. Bill Hagerty make a play for that plum seat if Grenell gets National Security Advisor, instead? ( Doug Burgum is eyeing the top diplomatic post, too\xe2\x80\xa6) Trump seems to be moving quickly. Late today, he announced Susie Wiles as chief of staff. Watch this space\xe2\x80\xa6

Tonight, I\xe2\x80\x99ve got some fresh reporting on the remarkable debt the Harris campaign racked up in its final, frantic weeks, and how they plan to pay it down. Plus, dispatches from Abby Livingston on the midterm hype already building around Abigail Spanberger (the Dems\xe2\x80\x99 Glenn Youngkin ?) and Mike Lawler (the next Lee Zeldin ?) and a post- Selzer post-mortem from John Heilemann .

But first\xe2\x80\xa6 a brief 2024 autopsy from a veteran G.O.P. strategist\xe2\x80\xa6

Why Harris Lost (According to Steve Schmidt)
On Wednesday, I sat down with veteran Republican strategist Steve Schmidt to discuss where the Harris campaign went wrong. The following has been excerpted from my podcast, Somebody\xe2\x80\x99s Gotta Win ...

I want to get into \xe2\x80\x9cwoulda, coulda, shoulda\xe2\x80\x9d in the Democratic Party. Should she have chosen Josh Shapiro, who won by more than 15 percentage points when he ran for governor of Pennsylvania? Democrats were privately worried about stoking the flames of the Gaza War by choosing a Jewish running mate\xe2\x80\x94was that a mistake?

I think Shapiro probably would\xe2\x80\x99ve helped them carry Pennsylvania. I\xe2\x80\x99m not ready to say that Gaza was determinative in Michigan against all the other trend lines. I think Shapiro was the strongest candidate and probably the best-prepared to be president of the United States. He was the best politician she could have picked, and she didn\xe2\x80\x99t, for whatever reason. But that pick didn\xe2\x80\x99t determine the outcome; it\xe2\x80\x99s on a list of 50 things. If we were ranking them today, her decision to choose Walz over Shapiro would be somewhere in the 30s.

If you were to rank them, what caused Kamala Harris to lose?

One of the things that happens in a political campaign is people look at an outcome and say, What is the biggest thing that happened closest to the outcome? And ergo, that\xe2\x80\x99s the biggest thing that caused the outcome.
Kamala Harris had the opportunity to walk in, say thank you to everybody who was working on the Biden campaign, clean house, make it clear that she was different, make it clear what she would\xe2\x80\x99ve done differently\xe2\x80\x94and she didn\xe2\x80\x99t do any of those things.

Not offending Biden\xe2\x80\x99s ego was more important [to the Harris campaign] than winning the election. She could have said, I was a new vice president and I watched how Afghanistan went down; that will never happen on my watch . She may have upset the man who picked her, but that\xe2\x80\x99s the cost of winning.

But if you look at the campaign, it was empty. It didn\xe2\x80\x99t speak to a momentous moment\xe2\x80\x94more than, at most, a time or two\xe2\x80\x94to anything grand, to anything consequential. There was a lot of banality in the campaign and a lot of \xe2\x80\x9cout of touchness\xe2\x80\x9d in the Democratic Party. And all of it is reinforced by this cacophony of media pundits who literally all live within a 3-square-mile block, in a small geography within a giant country.

Harris didn\xe2\x80\x99t do as much press as many people wanted. Why did they wait so long\xe2\x80\x94I think it was 30 or 40 days into the campaign\xe2\x80\x94to do their first interview?

Even then, they got pushed into it because of media pressure. It looked like they broke. Listen, I would\xe2\x80\x99ve had a different media strategy. But the bottom line is this: There\xe2\x80\x99s no way you get through a presidential campaign without talking to the media\xe2\x80\x94and if you try to, it makes you look weak, makes you look like you\xe2\x80\x99re hiding something. And against someone like Trump, it was not the right thing to do.

The American people can tell when a candidate doesn\xe2\x80\x99t have a good story for why they should be president, or what their vision is. And I think that\xe2\x80\x99s what we saw on election night.

And the price is loss. There\xe2\x80\x99s a price that has come due for all the pretending, all the evasions, all the lunacy of Biden was as good as he\xe2\x80\x99s ever been; he\xe2\x80\x99s ready to go; he\xe2\x80\x99s ready to serve until he\xe2\x80\x99s 86 . And so, his selfishness choked off a process that could have resulted in a winning candidate, and instead resulted in this.

Now, here\xe2\x80\x99s Abby with the view inside the Capitol\xe2\x80\xa6
Spanberger, Lawler & Way-Too-Early \xe2\x80\x9926 Fantasies
Yes, we\xe2\x80\x99re in the still-warm ashes of the 2024 election, but party strategists are already plotting and fretting about 2026. Republicans are on an incredible high after capturing the presidency, the Senate, and likely the House, but they know the midterms will be stacked against them. An unshackled Trump will almost certainly reignite enthusiasm on the left, and historically speaking, Republican candidates don\xe2\x80\x99t fare as well when he\xe2\x80\x99s not on the ballot. The 2018 Democratic landslide remains a fresh memory.

Democrats may be apoplectic and livid with one another, but that won\xe2\x80\x99t last long. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m focused on the midterms,\xe2\x80\x9d one beleaguered Democratic strategist texted me yesterday. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd how many Democrats we can elect in 730 days to hamper Trump for his last two years.\xe2\x80\x9d Here are the bellwether races that the pros are already watching\xe2\x80\xa6

  • The Spanberger signal : Over the last decade or so, political operatives have frequently looked to Virginia\xe2\x80\x94a mostly Democratic state that experiences Republican mood swings every few years\xe2\x80\x94as an early indicator of national political trends. And, thanks in part to its infamous off-year election, it is always viewed as a referendum on the incumbent president\xe2\x80\x99s performance. To wit: Glenn Youngkin \xe2\x80\x99s upset victory over Terry McAuliffe in 2021 turned out to be a harbinger of Joe Biden\xe2\x80\x99s broader challenges.

    That\xe2\x80\x99s why Democrats will be watching particularly closely when Rep. Abigail Spanberger retires at the end of this year to run for Virginia governor in 2025. She\xe2\x80\x99s already considered a strong candidate, having spent this cycle raising piles of money for state legislature candidates. Just as important, she made sure that her successor was a Democrat. Eugene Vindman \xe2\x80\x99s three-point win over the highly touted Republican candidate, retired Green Beret Derrick Anderson , was one of the few pleasant surprises for Democrats from Tuesday night.

    But the real reason Spanberger\xe2\x80\x99s campaign matters for Democrats is because she\xe2\x80\x99ll likely be test-piloting new messaging for the party. Back in 2020, she made waves among her colleagues with her vocal opposition to Democrats indulging far-left rallying cries like \xe2\x80\x9cdefund the police,\xe2\x80\x9d which she dismissed with expletive-laden exasperation . A former C.I.A. and law enforcement officer, Spanberger is already being looked to as a litmus test for how a more centrist flavor of Democratic politics could play in a state that\xe2\x80\x99s still blue\xe2\x80\x94but now, just barely.

  • Lawler\xe2\x80\x99s big night : On the Republican side, the most watched member could end up being Mike Lawler , who just secured a second term in New York\xe2\x80\x99s moderate-ish 17th District. Few in Congress had a better night than the Republican freshman incumbent, who defeated Democrat Mondaire Jones by 25,000 votes. (Lawler\xe2\x80\x99s margin of victory in his 2022 race against then-D.C.C.C. chairman Sean Patrick Maloney was just 1,200 votes.) Lawler\xe2\x80\x99s margin this time was large enough that his name is circulating as a challenger to Democratic Gov. Kathy Hochul in 2026.

    Lawler\xe2\x80\x99s decision probably depends on whether Hochul runs for reelection. But if he does take on Albany, it\xe2\x80\x99ll be on very different terms than when Lee Zeldin came within seven points of Hochul in 2022. Next cycle, there will be no Chuck Schumer or Kirsten Gillibrand on the ballot. And Lawler may be in a position to uplift fellow Republicans down-ballot, too. Much like the last two cycles, control for the House in 2026 may come down to the Empire State, which Trump lost by a much smaller margin this time\xe2\x80\x9412 points against Harris vs. 24 points against Biden.
And John Heilemann with the view inside Harris HQ on that misleading Iowa poll\xe2\x80\xa6
The Selzer Reality Check
In a presidential election that was, by a wide margin, the most frequently and heavily polled\xe2\x80\x94or, depending on your P.O.V., ridiculously over -polled\xe2\x80\x94race for the White House in history, no single public-opinion survey made as big a splash as the final installment of the Des Moines Register /Mediacom Iowa Poll. Conducted by the legendary polling maestro J. Ann Selzer , the Iowa Poll sent the political world into a full-blown, top-to-bottom tizzy when it dropped last Saturday night and showed Kamala Harris with a three-point lead, 47-44 percent, over Donald Trump .

One reason Selzer\xe2\x80\x99s poll landed like a lightning bolt was that, given the fire-engine-redness of the Hawkeye State, its findings were so unlikely. Another was that the poll was at such flagrant odds with both the polling consensus in the state, which put Trump ahead by seven points, and the blizzard of homestretch polls that found the race to be statistically tied in all seven battleground states, as well as nationally. Finally and most important, there was Selzer\xe2\x80\x99s vaunted reputation for analytical rigor and domain expertise, which over the course of her long and storied career had led her to drop polls semi-routinely that were mocked as egregious outliers\xe2\x80\x94only to be proven right in the end.

In the end, of course, the perception that Harris might have understated strength with white women, independent voters, and seniors turned out to be just another of the Democratic illusions that were shattered into a million little pieces by the results on Tuesday night: Not only did Harris not win Iowa, she didn\xe2\x80\x99t even make it close.

Selzer is far from the only pollster who has some explaining to do in the wake of Trump\xe2\x80\x99s sweeping, comprehensive, realignment-cementing victory. For the third straight presidential election cycle, the polling industrial complex has emerged with a jumbo omelette\xe2\x80\x99s worth of egg all over its face. Sure, the polling averages for the Blue Wall states correctly forecast that all three were on a knife\xe2\x80\x99s edge and could go narrowly either way\xe2\x80\x94though they made Pennsylvania, North Carolina, and Georgia seem closer than they proved to be, and appear to have missed fairly dramatically in Arizona and Nevada. And sure, Nate Silver and others warned us repeatedly that a Trump sweep of all seven battlegrounds wasn\xe2\x80\x99t just conceivable but quite likely.

But the headline national polling, which is what ordinary readers and viewers pay the most attention to, was almost as far off in 2024 as it was in 2020 and 2016\xe2\x80\x94two of the most embarrassing collective polling cock-ups of all time. As Ed Kilgore noted in New York , Harris led in all the national polling averages on election eve. Whereas in reality, at this hour, Trump is ahead of Harris 50.9 to 47.6 percent in the national popular vote.

No doubt this raises many questions, not least for the reporters, analysts, and political junkies who have come to invest way too much importance and blind faith in political polling. But it should also lead to the most serious and sustained round of self-scrutiny yet by pollsters, themselves\xe2\x80\x94who could do a lot worse channeling Ann Selzer, whose response to her rare L showed her to be a consummate pro and class act. \xe2\x80\x9c[Our] poll findings \xe2\x80\xa6 did not match what the Iowa electorate ultimately decided,\xe2\x80\x9d Selzer said in a statement. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ll be reviewing data from multiple sources with hopes of learning why that happened. And I welcome what that process might teach me.\xe2\x80\x9d

And now, those Kamala finances\xe2\x80\xa6
The Kamala Inc. Fire Sale
The Harris financial juggernaut, which raised well over $1 billion, is still soliciting cash and has begun seeking buyers for its email lists in order to stave off its debts\xe2\x80\x94raising uncomfortable questions about where and how all that money was spent in the first place.
TARA PALMERI
There were many historic aspects of Kamala Harris \xe2\x80\x99s candidacy: her gender, her race, her midsummer anointment. But perhaps most jaw-dropping was the incredible success of her fundraising machine, which amassed more than $1 billion in donations, an unprecedented sum that was tangible, undeniable proof of her campaign\xe2\x80\x99s momentum and enthusiasm.

But within 24 hours of her landslide loss, as anyone who received an election night text from \xe2\x80\x9cBarack Obama\xe2\x80\x9d probably sussed out, the campaign was in the red. Today, word started to trickle out that the campaign has essentially burned its entire cash reserve, and is still fundraising to cover potential debts, an equally stunning data point for an operation that had as much as $118 million in cash on hand as of October 16.

Contributions are still trickling in, according to a D.N.C. source, who said the campaign\xe2\x80\x99s total debt will almost certainly be lower once all the incoming money is accounted for. (They have until December 15 to submit their fourth-quarter campaign finance report.) Nevertheless, I\xe2\x80\x99m told by campaign sources, the Harris camp is now reaching out to Democratic groups to sell her vast dataset of supporter emails and phone numbers to offset those losses\xe2\x80\x94a common tactic among both Democrats and Republicans. (Yes, Obama did it too\xe2\x80\xa6) How valuable is Harris\xe2\x80\x99s supporter list? Given donor fatigue at both the small-dollar and high-dollar donor levels, any buyer may find them already strip-mined of much of their value.

The Harris campaign is pursuing other tactics, too. It has already launched a post-election fundraising campaign on KamalaHarris.com called the \xe2\x80\x9cHarris Fight Fund,\xe2\x80\x9d whose stated mission is to maintain staffing in states where Senate and House races are still too close to call. Texts from \xe2\x80\x9c Tim Walz \xe2\x80\x9d were still requesting $40 for the Harris Fight Fund a full two days after the polls closed. Whatever donations \xe2\x80\x9cTim\xe2\x80\x9d or any of the other solicitors collect goes to the Democratic National Committee, to offset the campaign debt they\xe2\x80\x99ll inherit.

These financial pressures are likely to factor into December\xe2\x80\x99s election for D.N.C. chairperson. But first, the organization is likely to lay off some staff, as is typical following an election. Current chair Jaime Harrison is expected to step down, leaving his successor to somehow refortify the group\xe2\x80\x99s tattered balance sheet\xe2\x80\x94not an easy job when many are questioning the efficacy of text outreach and email, not to mention the efficacy of the Democratic Party itself. \xe2\x80\x9cThe chair is going to have to spend 75 percent of their time fundraising in an ecosystem where donors are fatigued, and they\xe2\x80\x99re going to be in deep financial trouble,\xe2\x80\x9d said a former D.N.C. official. (A spokesperson for the D.N.C. declined to comment.)
The Money Pit
As the shell shock of Trump\xe2\x80\x99s victory slowly wears off, the post-election quarterbacking, intraparty sniping, and digital shit-talking among Democrats is quickly surpassing 2016 levels, which were historic, if you recall. \xe2\x80\x9cInstead of owning any mistakes, or being transparent about the voter data and strategies that were so obviously wrong, they shut off their Twitter account and are patting each other on the back,\xe2\x80\x9d said a former D.N.C. consultant, taking a swipe at top Harris advisor David Plouffe , who deleted his social media profile after posting, \xe2\x80\x9cWe dug out of a deep hole but not enough.\xe2\x80\x9d

The perceived mismanagement of the Democrats\xe2\x80\x99 financial juggernaut has instigated plenty of finger-pointing, too. \xe2\x80\x9cWe spent money in stupid ways because we had a really bad strategy,\xe2\x80\x9d said the former official, pointing to the money the D.N.C. lavished on Colin Allred \xe2\x80\x99s long-shot Senate race in Texas, which he lost by nearly nine points, and the late decision to move money into Iowa, another state that the party hasn\xe2\x80\x99t won since 2012.

Obviously, Harris is hardly the first presidential candidate to go into debt. Hillary Clinton ended her 2008 campaign owing about $25 million. What\xe2\x80\x99s riling up some D.N.C. insiders now is the allegation that they were left largely in the dark about the 2024 budget. The numbers were closely guarded by Biden campaign manager Jen O\xe2\x80\x99Malley Dillon , who stayed on with Harris. I\xe2\x80\x99m told even Harris didn\xe2\x80\x99t know she was in debt, which certainly makes sense in the final weeks of a down-to-the-wire, high anxiety race. (Perhaps better to run into debt and win, the thinking goes, than lose because you\xe2\x80\x99re being too frugal.) \xe2\x80\x9cThey never shared the budget when they were asked,\xe2\x80\x9d a source familiar with the finances said. A D.N.C. official said that the majority of money spent was on major events, paid media, and the campaign\xe2\x80\x99s much-discussed, supposedly formidable ground game.

Of course, there\xe2\x80\x99s still plenty of blame to go around. Other Democrats are funneling their ire toward Future Forward, the Democratic super PAC that raised a staggering $700 million in combination with its nonprofit group. In the campaign\xe2\x80\x99s closing weeks, the group funded a massive digital advertising campaign that may have contributed to the sense of momentum Harris enjoyed in the final sprint, but which was clearly not effective enough. In any case, said a D.N.C. member, \xe2\x80\x9cI hope this is the end of the people who keep losing every campaign.\xe2\x80\x9d
